report_name: Tycoon 2FA Phishing-as-a-Service Platform Bypasses MFA Using Real Microsoft Login Pages

threat_actors.name: Tycoon 2FA Operators
threat_actors.description: Low-skilled or non-technical criminals who use the Tycoon 2FA Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS) platform to conduct adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) attacks to bypass MFA and compromise Microsoft 365 accounts.

attack_patterns.name: Spearphishing Link
attack_patterns.description: The attack begins with a phishing email containing a link to the phishing site. In some cases, the link is embedded within a malicious calendar invite (.ics file) to bypass email security filters.

attack_patterns.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle
attack_patterns.description: The core of the attack involves a reverse proxy server that fetches the real Microsoft login page in real-time and presents it to the user, allowing the platform to intercept the authentication flow and session token.

attack_patterns.name: Steal Web Session Cookie
attack_patterns.description: The primary goal of the Tycoon 2FA platform is to intercept and steal the session cookie generated after a successful user login and MFA challenge, which is then used to gain unauthorized access to the victim's Microsoft 365 account.

attack_patterns.name: Multi-Factor Authentication Request Generation
attack_patterns.description: The adversary-in-the-middle platform proxies the legitimate MFA challenge from Microsoft to the user. When the user approves the request, the subsequent session token is stolen by the attacker.

malware.name: Tycoon 2FA
malware.description: A Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS) platform that enables adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) attacks to bypass MFA on Microsoft 365 accounts by using a reverse proxy to steal session cookies.

malware.name: EvilTokens
malware.description: A Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS) platform mentioned alongside Tycoon 2FA that democratizes advanced AiTM attacks to steal session tokens and bypass MFA.

identities.name: Microsoft 365 Users
identities.description: Users of Microsoft 365 services are the primary targets of the Tycoon 2FA phishing campaign.

infrastructure.name: Tycoon 2FA Phishing Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: A reverse proxy server that presents a real Microsoft login page to victims in order to intercept and steal session cookies after a successful MFA-enabled login.

indicators.name: Suspicious ICS File in Email Attachment
indicators.pattern:
title: Suspicious ICS File in Email Attachment
id: 2b1a9c8d-4e5f-4a3b-8c7d-6e5f4a3b2c1d
status: experimental
description: Detects incoming emails with .ics calendar file attachments, which can be used as a lure in phishing campaigns like Tycoon 2FA.
references:
  - https://blog.barracuda.com/2026/06/29/email-threat-radar-june-2026
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-30
tags:
  - attack.t1566.002
level: medium
logsource:
  category: email
detection:
  selection:
    Attachment-Filename|endswith: '.ics'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects emails containing .ics calendar invites, which are used in the Tycoon 2FA campaign to deliver the phishing link and bypass some security filters.

indicators.name: Anomalous Microsoft 365 Sign-in
indicators.pattern:
title: Anomalous Microsoft 365 Sign-in from Non-Compliant Device
id: 9d8c7b6a-5f4e-3d2c-1b0a-987654321fed
status: experimental
description: 'Detects successful Microsoft 365 sign-ins from devices that are not compliant with the organization''s policy. This can be an indicator of a session token stolen via an AiTM attack being used on an attacker''s machine.'
references:
  - https://blog.barracuda.com/2026/06/29/email-threat-radar-june-2026
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-30
tags:
  - attack.t1539
level: high
logsource:
  product: azure
  service: signinlogs
detection:
  selection:
    ResultType: '0'
    ConditionalAccessStatus: 'success'
    DeviceDetail.isCompliant: 'false'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects successful Microsoft Entra ID sign-ins from non-compliant devices, which could indicate the use of a stolen session cookie by an attacker.

indicators.name: Potential AiTM Phishing Page Access
indicators.pattern:
title: Potential Microsoft AiTM Phishing Page Access
id: 1a2b3c4d-5e6f-7a8b-9c0d-1e2f3a4b5c6d
status: experimental
description: 'Detects potential user access to an adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) phishing page impersonating Microsoft by looking for POST requests to non-Microsoft domains that contain login-related keywords.'
references:
  - https://blog.barracuda.com/2026/06/29/email-threat-radar-june-2026
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-30
tags:
  - attack.t1557
level: medium
logsource:
  category: proxy
detection:
  selection:
    cs-method: 'POST'
    c-uri|contains:
      - 'login'
      - 'password'
      - 'microsoft'
      - 'office365'
  filter:
    cs-host|endswith:
      - '.microsoft.com'
      - '.live.com'
      - '.microsoftonline.com'
  condition: selection and not filter
indicators.description: Detects when a user accesses a non-Microsoft domain that appears to be a Microsoft login page, a strong indicator of an AiTM phishing attempt.

relationships.source_name: Suspicious ICS File in Email Attachment
